danger_signals.signal: Obstacles and gates blocking forward progress
danger_signals.visual_form: Physical barriers on playfield that must be navigated or cleared
danger_signals.meaning: Impassable terrain requiring puzzle-solving or timing to bypass

danger_signals.visual_form: Timed sequences requiring coordination with R.O.B. or Controller 2 player
danger_signals.meaning: Risk of failure if actions are not synchronized or completed within window

safety_signals.visual_form: Open routes on playfield after gates are moved
safety_signals.meaning: Progress possible when obstacles removed by cooperative action

safety_signals.visual_form: Coordinated movement between player avatar and R.O.B./second controller
safety_signals.meaning: Safe progression when both actors move in harmony

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Physical robot body with working arms
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Physical peripheral device; in-game representation controlling gate mechanisms

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Closed gates visible on playfield, creating impassable barriers
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must wait for R.O.B. or second controller to manipulate gate before advancing

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dual-character coordination display showing both player and R.O.B. actions simultaneously
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game requires synchronized timing between human player and robot (or two human players); failure occurs if coordination breaks

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Same playfield but gate control delegated to second controller rather than physical robot
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Fast-paced gameplay due to immediate controller response versus R.O.B.'s noted slowness

environmental_storytelling.example: Gates that must be physically moved by R.O.B. arm mechanism tell story of automation and robotic assistance in puzzle-solving
Custom playfield environment reinforces R.O.B.'s role as integral to progression rather than optional gimmick
Game structure (gates controlled separately from player movement) communicates interdependence between human and robot entities

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Moves gates via mechanical arm manipulation; responds to on-screen flash signals from NES output; motion is notably slow and deliberate
Fails to respond to flash signals if optical alignment is poor or flashes are not clearly visible; creates unwinnable state requiring synchronization reset
Cannot advance beyond closed gates; movement is blocked until R.O.B. completes gate manipulation sequence
Free to move forward; progression becomes possible after cooperative action sequence completes

notes: Gyromite is fundamentally a cooperative puzzle-action game that communicates world-state through physical obstacle presence and absence. The game's core visual language centers on the R.O.B. peripheral as a collaborative agent—gates and barriers visually encode the necessity of human-robot coordination. The playset environment itself becomes a manifestation of the game's core mechanic: dual-agent control. Notably, the sources emphasize that Gyromite could be played without R.O.B. by substituting Controller 2, revealing that the visual/mechanical communication system is robust enough to function in both contexts. The game's danger signals are largely temporal (time pressure) and spatial (obstacle placement) rather than aesthetic (no traditional enemy factions or threat visualization). This reflects the 1985 action-puzzle paradigm where danger emerges from puzzle complexity and coordination failure rather than hostile agents.
